speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter and-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In Kiev, were you able to observe your Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no, but I did it, only on the high holidays and on Pesach. On Passover, OK, I used to have matzos. And let me tell you, in my family, I was the only religious man in my family. So they see-- I want them to know our Jewish holidays, what kind of holidays they are. And I want them to-- to know who they are, who we are. You can't escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were your children born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born in 1950, in November 1950. She's going to be 45. My son was born in 1955, in-- in August. So they finished high school, both of them. Then, they went to universities. My daughter finished as accounting, and he's an engineer, a power engineer, or let's say, mechanical engineer. So he worked here as an engineer. And now, he's in Ann Arbor. He got a good position. He is director of energy department there, in a-- in a big company-- what's it called, the company? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Edison Electric. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name is? Your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son's name is Leonard, Lenny. So he lived there, and he's doing good. My daughter finished her special-- special school as a-- a computer-- a computer programmer. But she worked just a short time. And now she runs here on a travel agency, and she's doing OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you arrive in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In September 1979, but my daughter arrives earlier in March the-- the same year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how has it been for you in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me tell you. I'm always happy, despite my family wasn't happy. It was different periods, you know? But I was always happy. And-- and so is now. Now, everyone is happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was the leader.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of being happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of being happy, of coming to the United States despite my age. I was 63. I-- I didn't think at that time I-- I can get a job in here, but I did. I worked for 10 years, like I told you, here for a company as an engineer. One-- nine months I worked as a draftsman, and then they gave me an engineering position, and I was happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what company did you work for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Astronautics of America. It's an electronic company. It's a-- a Jewish company. Zelazo, if you know-- if you hear about it. Nate Zelazo. It's his company. He's the president. He was the president. Now his son is the president.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think back on your-- the experiences that you had, if you could leave a message to, or have final thoughts for your children, or for the world, what-- what might you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my children, to keep their eyes open, to be on watch. Now, there are neo-Nazis here, even in the United States, and all over the world. They are something new like militia. They are anti-Semitic too. There are some [INAUDIBLE] that are anti-Semitic too. So we've got enemies from the right and from the left. So we got to always to keep our eyes open, and that's it. And then, we-- we got to always help and to-- and to think of the state of Israel. It's our state. It's a Jewish state. As long as the Jewish state will exist, we are saved. That's what I-- I am saying. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and in the end, I want to-- I am very happy. I was nervous, you know, a little nervous before that, but I'm happy now. I told my story, and I want to thank you, Sandy, particular you, and your team. And I want to thank-- to thank also Steven Spielberg, because he-- he did it, he created. He's a smart guy. He's a good guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: We want to thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he did this foundation. I want to thank him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we want to thank you, and now, we're gonna look at your photographs. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my family, actually not in full. My son is not here. My wife, Rita, my daughter, Inna, and my granddaughter, Jackie. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Jackie, and I'm in fifth grade, and I like the olden days. And I am, like, interested, like, where my grandfather was born, and-- and I would like to go there one day. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: What can you say about your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he's really nice. And he knows Hebrew. And-- and he goes to synagogue with me, and that's mostly it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we thank you. I understand that this was interview about my father's life, and as-- as we all know and I'm sure you understand, that he got-- he went through a lot in his life. He endured a lot. And we're really glad and happy that he is with us. And after he went through a very long and hard period of his life, he is now well, healthy, energetic. And we thank you, all of you, that you were interested in his past, and you came to interview him and shared his experience. And I hope that he can not only share his experience with all of us, but also with hopefully, many other people that will be watching that particular tape, or part of that particular tape. And it will be registered somewhere in the history. We thank you again for coming. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, on this picture, my father, Gersh, my mother, Ita, and myself. I believe, I am here nine years old. And what's the occasion for taking this picture? It seems to me because we used to-- to mail it to-- to America, to-- to the sisters and brothers. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my picture. It was taken right after the war end in 1945. I was 29 years old, and I was in the army as a sergeant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want to point it out on the person who is on the left. This is Ivan Nikolai Schwitz. He's a Ukraine. He was a good friend of mine, a very good friend. I told you before, when it was a critical situation, he brought me a book, a soldier's book, a plain book, and said if the Germans would-- could capture us, you got this. Fill out any name what we want. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my wife's and my marriage certificate. It happened in the year-- 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1949. [LAUGHS] In July, the 22nd of July. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my daughter, Inna. You just saw her. I believe she was nine years old. And at the left, my son, [? Loynia, ?] Lenny, or Leonard, he is now, and probably was four years old here. And myself. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Kiev in 1979 before we left the Soviet Union. The problem was I couldn't take legal all these medals, so that's why I decided to make a picture before throwing them out. And what I brought, I-- I show you. It's only three of them, the main significant medals. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my wife and I, and I believe it was taken seven years ago here in this room, and it was-- it was a holiday. [LAUGHS] I don't remember what kind of holiday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, definitely it was a holiday. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture, I believe, was taken in 1979, in April probably or May, after my daughter arrived here in Milwaukee. You can see my nephew at the rear right side. Paul [INAUDIBLE], my daughter, Inna, my sister, Gitl, and Paul's late wife, Gitl. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this is a really cute picture. It was taken last April, this year, in Ann Arbor, when Jessica my granddaughter was born. She was born in April-- in March 27. And this is, I think she's two-- two weeks old, maybe three. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my son, Lenny, my daughter, Inna, and my granddaughter, Jackie. You just saw them. And it was taken here in this room last-- last in January or-- or December, in December. I believe, in December last year. This is one of my three brothers from my father's side. His name was [? Bertric, ?] Boris. And this is my niece, but she's a daughter of another brother. He died before the war, Schmuel. This picture, I believe, was taken in 1956. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, that's it. This is my sister, Sonya, and her husband, my brother-in-law, [? Mordecai. ?] It was taken in Israel in years, I believe in the '50s. They had been on a seminar there. This is a visa. It was given by the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ukraine or Kiev [? avair ?]. They called it [? avair ?] for-- to immigrate to the United States. It cost me a lot of nerve to get it. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: [POINTING] Here, at the city of Rovno, actually near the city of Rovno, in [INAUDIBLE] was deployed the-- the force artillery, Germans, where I used to-- to serve my duty. And here started the war. From here, we are traded with battles, back till [INAUDIBLE], and here we stayed for, I believe, six weeks. Near this corridor, we went this way, until here, until the army was defeated and fell apart. From here, like I-- like I told you, I changed my clothes to civil clothes, and went this way, this way, this way, this way, this way, and it-- it took me 18 months to do that. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] [POINTING] After I was released by the Red Army, I went from here, from, this is approximately the village of [INAUDIBLE] to-- to Valuyki. Here is Valuyki. From here to Valuyki. From Valuyki, I got the notice to go to my company, which was deployed in Merefa, here, near Kharkov. And from here, again, there's battles. The offensive was go this way. Again, we cross the River Dnieper here, we go-- we are going further this way. Near [INAUDIBLE], it was a great, great battle in here. And the German Army was defeated again in here. Then, we went this way. And here we are in Bessarabia Village. It's a city in Bessarabia. Again, we went this way. Here we are in Romania. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Here we are in Romania, and then, this is Hungary. This way Hungary. From Hungary, [POINTING] we went to Austria, to Vienna. I was there. And then from Vienna, we went a little here to the-- to the Swiss border. And we stopped in here, and that was the end of the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Those are some of medals I deserve-- I received for participating in battles during the war, during this period from March 1943 till the end of the war in May 1945. This is the highest medal. [POINTING] They called it Warden of the Red Star. [POINTING] This is a medal of participating in the war. Sorry, no, this a medal also for participating in the battles. And this is a medal of at the end of the war, it was given for participating of four years in the war. I got some other medals, but [LAUGHS] unfortunately, I throwed them away, because I couldn't take them. Those are-- [POINTING] are taken illegal, just illegal to-- to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.
